abstract: Self-assembling molecular drugs offer a unique middle ground between the straightforward synthesis of small-molecule chemotherapeutics and the precise targeting capabilities of nanoparticle conjugates. However, their clinical utility has been limited by the difficulty of maintaining stable supramolecular interactions within the complex physiological environment of a living organism. In this study, we demonstrate that metallophilic interactions between cyclometalated palladium complexes can drive the formation of supramolecular nanostructures that remain stable in vivo. In a skin melanoma mouse model, these assemblies exhibited an extended circulation half-life exceeding 12 hours and achieved a remarkable tumor accumulation rate of 10.2% of the injected dose per gram. Upon activation with green light, the self-assembled complexes triggered a potent type I photodynamic effect, resulting in significant tumor regression and an up to 96-fold increase in cytotoxicity compared to dark conditions. These findings establish metallophilic interactions as a robust platform for engineering stable, high-performance supramolecular nanotherapeutics with superior tumor-targeting profiles.
